translation.content: "High, Sammy, look up high, Sammy." Originally heard in Dutch crooner Ramses Shaffy's 1966 hit "Sammy," this lyric inspired Dutch DJ and producer Thessa Torsing to adopt her alias, upsammy. And true to the name, she always aims high. Since her breakout in 2017, Torsing has built an enviable CV: a debut release on Nous'klaer Audio, an album on Dekmantel and a residency at former Amsterdam clubbing institution De School. Over these years, she's honed a singular sound, navigating wild variations in tempo and mood that dance along the edges of techno, IDM and drum & bass (we could list many more, but you know the drill). Her creative arc has seen her delve into ever more abstract concepts, such as 2024's ambient album, Strange Meridians, exploring the interplay between synthetic and natural textures through drumless experimentations. As she explained in her 2018 Breaking Through profile, "I just want to hear the weirdness in the music." Torsing's RA Podcast showcases exactly why she's one of the most consistently adventurous names in dance music. Describing it as "a mix with a narrative," the former landscape architecture student flexes her ability to build sonic environments, beginning with sounds more akin to a swamp than a club. Across 90 minutes, she moves through numerous layered terrains, exploring everything from Skee Mask's lucid, beatless techno to the piercing acid of Mike Parker. Unfolding with a restless sense of curiosity, RA.970 captures an artist challenging the boundaries of electronic music, ever upward. – Bella Aquilina What have you been up to recently? Last year I had many great and adventurous experiences at festivals like Waking Life, Dripping, Unsound. I've done different live shows, with Jonathan Castro and Valentina Magaletti. Released a new record. I've also started collaborations with other artists, which has been a wonderful experience, since being a producer can be quite a secluded lifestyle. I'm starting the new year in an exploratory mode, opening my ears to new sounds and ideas. How and where was the mix recorded? And can you tell us the idea behind it? This mix was recorded at home on three CDJs, a Xone:92 mixer and an FX pedal. I wanted to make a mix with a narrative. Something that slowly builds up in intensity, towards a more ravey moment and then also the melancholic moment afterwards. My idea was to incorporate melodies, voices and strange layers throughout, to create a contrasting patchwork of introspective and more bodily sensations. What are you looking forward to in the near future? Listening to new things, going on sonic adventures, working on new music and exchanging ideas with other like-minded artists. What's one social or political cause you want the world to pay more attention to? The war in Gaza, the illegal occupation of the West Bank and the war in Ukraine. We have a very populist, right-wing government in the Netherlands that's looking away from what's going on in Gaza and using people's fear to gain power. What's one club or party that had a major impact on you as an artist? Both De School and Garage Noord have had a profound impact on me. At both venues, I've felt truly at home—free to experiment and grow. These spaces have allowed me to explore many facets of being an artist: curating nights, performing live shows, playing in every possible time slot and engaging in adventurous back-to-backs. As a clubber, I've experienced a deep sense of belonging, meeting weird and wonderful people along the way. It's hard to believe that it's been nearly eight years since I first started playing at these places.
translation.blurb: The Dekmantel favourite kicks off 2025 in an exploratory mood.
